original_abstract:
Proton-exchanged zeolites are among the most important industrial catalysts, yet unravelling the atomic-scale mechanisms of the chemical transformations they facilitate remains a significant challenge. This review provides a comprehensive molecular perspective on the mechanisms governing Brønsted acid-catalyzed reactions in zeolites, emphasizing the intricate interplay between acid site nature and the role of spatial confinement. By synthesizing findings from advanced kinetic modeling, in situ and operando spectroscopies, and high-level quantum chemical calculations, we examine the behavior of key intermediates—specifically carbenium and carbonium ions—across a diverse array of substrates. 

A detailed focus is given to the conversion of hydrocarbons, including alkanes, alkenes, and aromatics, alongside the dehydration of alcohols and polyhydroxy molecules like sugars. We discuss fundamental elementary steps such as C-C, C-H, and C-O bond breaking and formation, highlighting how the local zeolite environment stabilizes transition states and dictates product selectivity. Furthermore, the review addresses the impact of extra-framework species, defects, and the specific role of the external surface. By identifying remaining gaps in our understanding, we provide a roadmap for future research directed toward the rational design of more efficient and selective zeolite catalysts for applications in refining, petrochemistry, and the conversion of emerging bio-based feedstocks.
humanized_abstract:
Proton-exchanged zeolites are widely used in the petroleum and chemical industries. Despite their widespread use, very little is known in detail about the actual molecular events that occur during catalysis. In this review we summarise our present understanding of the mechanism of Brønsted acid catalysed reactions in zeolites. Our primary focus is the relationship between the structure of the active site and the role of the zeolite void space, for which we draw together information from modelling studies, in situ and operando spectroscopy, and theoretical simulation. The behaviour of the reaction-intermediates carbenium and carbonium ions in different reactions has been investigated in detail.

Hydrocarbon conversion, including conversion of alkanes, alkenes, and aromatics, as well as dehydration of alcohols and polyhydroxy species such as sugars is extensively discussed. Reaction mechanisms are inferred from modeling studies focusing on elementary steps such as C-C, C-H and C-O bond breaking and forming and are supported by an understanding of the role of the zeolite local structure in stabilizing transition states and influencing selectivity. The influence of extra-framework species, defects and surface properties is also discussed. The review highlights the gaps in present knowledge and sets out an agenda for optimizing zeolite catalyst structure and performance for the many applications of the petroleum industry ranging from refining and petrochemistry to biochemistry and bio-refining.
